title: Shortcut to Adiabatic Isomeric Population Transfer of the $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ Nucleus via Hyperfine Electronic Bridge
authors: Bo Liu, Wu Wang, Yong Li
abstract: The $^{229}$Th nucleus is well known for its exceptionally low-lying nuclear isomeric level, which provides a unique platform for exploring electron-nucleus interactions and gives rise to a variety of rich physical phenomena. One such phenomenon is the hyperfine electronic bridge, which has recently been shown to enable efficient and precise manipulation of the nuclear isomeric levels of $^{229}$Th [W. Wang $et~ al.$, Phys. Rev. Lett. \textbf{133}, 223001 (2024)]. However, that study used the stimulated Raman adiabatic passage method, which requires relatively long operation times. In this work, we employ the stimulated Raman shortcut-to-adiabatic passage method, which dramatically shortens the operation time from the order of hundreds of milliseconds to hundreds of microseconds while maintaining a transfer efficiency of about $79.38\%$.
summary: This paper introduces the Stimulated Raman Shortcut-to-Adiabatic Passage (STIRSAP) method for manipulating the isomeric state of the $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ nucleus. By employing counter-diabatic driving, the authors successfully accelerate the population transfer process from milliseconds down to microseconds. This advancement significantly enhances the feasibility of controlling nuclear isomers using electronic structure interactions.
main_problem: To significantly shorten the operation time for the isomeric population transfer of the $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ nucleus while maintaining high efficiency.
main_result: The Stimulated Raman Shortcut-to-Adiabatic Passage (STIRSAP) method achieves transfer in hundreds of microseconds, dramatically faster than previous methods, while maintaining high efficiency and showing improved robustness.
method: The study employs the STIRSAP quantum control technique, which utilizes counter-diabatic driving to accelerate the population transfer process compared to standard STIRAP.
model_or_system: The system is modeled as a six-level structure involving the $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ nucleus and its hyperfine electronic bridge. The time evolution is governed by a quantum master equation incorporating decay processes.
key_observables: Population transfer efficiency from the initial state to the isomeric state, and the operation time ($t_{	ext{total}}$).
important_parameters: Operation times (hundreds of microseconds vs. hundreds of milliseconds), two-photon detuning ($\delta$), and one-photon detuning ($\Delta$).
main_assumptions: The system is modeled as a six-level system, and the analysis assumes the initial state is prepared in the metastable $7s_{1/2}$ level.
figures_summary: Figures compare the efficiency of STIRSAP versus STIRAP as a function of total operation time, detuning, Rabi frequency, and two-photon detuning, demonstrating STIRSAP's superior speed and robustness.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the physical model and the need for faster control. It then derives the Hamiltonian and applies the STIRSAP technique using counter-diabatic driving. Finally, it systematically compares the results of STIRSAP against STIRAP across various parameter regimes (time, detuning) to prove its superiority.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics and open quantum systems as it applies advanced quantum control techniques (STAs/counter-diabatic driving) to manipulate nuclear quantum states using electronic structure as a bridge.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The six-level model for 229Th3+ ions begins with |1⟩≡|[Ig7s1/2]F = 2⟩and aims to excite the isomeric state |3⟩≡|[Ie7s1/2]F = 2⟩via the intermediate state |2⟩≡|[Ig7p1/2]F = 3⟩. An electron-assisting laser (with Rabi frequency Ωe) drives the transition between |1⟩and |2⟩, while a bridge-assisting laser (with Rabi frequency Ωb) couples |2⟩and |3⟩. The decay from |2⟩to |1⟩(with decay rate γ21), |4⟩≡|[Ig7s1/2]F = 3⟩(with decay rate γ24), and |5⟩≡|Ig, 6d3/2⟩(with decay rate γ25), as well as the decay from |5⟩to |6⟩≡|Ig, 5f5/2⟩(with decay rates γ56) are considered. Hyperfine structures of |5⟩and |6⟩are ignored, as they do not affect the isomeric population transfer from |1⟩to |3⟩. ∆= E2...

Fig. 2. The upper and lower panels correspond to total operation times of ttotal = 280 µs and ttotal = 140 µs, respectively. (a, d) Rabi frequencies ˜Ωe(t) and ˜Ωb(t) employed in the STIRSAP scheme. (b, e) Population transfer efficiencies from state |1⟩to state |3⟩achieved via the STIRSAP scheme, which are 79.38% and 35.63%, respectively. (c, f) Population transfer efficiencies obtained with the STIRAP scheme, corresponding to 41.88% and 0.36%, respectively. The parameters are: (a, b) σ = 54 µs, te = 120 µs, tb = 160 µs, Ωb/2π = Ωe/2π = 6.8×105 Hz, ∆= 1000|Ωb(tb)|; (c) σ = 67 µs, te = 120 µs, tb = 122 µs, Ωb/2π = Ωe/2π = 12.9 × 105 Hz, ∆= 1000|Ωb(tb)|; (d, b) σ = 20 µs, te = 67 µs, tb = 73...

Fig. 3. (a) Influence of the total operation time ttotal on the population transfer efficiency of the STIRSAP scheme (red dashed line) and the STIRAP scheme (blue solid line), respectively. Parameters are set as Ωb(tb)/2π = Ωe(te)/2π = 6.8×105 Hz and ∆= 1000 |Ωb(tb)|. (b, c) Influence of the detuning ∆on the population transfer efficiency, comparing the STIRSAP (red dashed line) and the STIRAP scheme (blue solid line). Parameters are set as Ωb(tb)/2π = Ωe(te)/2π = 6.8 × 105 Hz. The total operation times are 140 ms for (b) and 140 µs for (c), respectively.
